title: Sessionless Exec
body:
### Feature request description

Most usage of Podman in HPC environments focuses on a single container for each user. This container is run only once and provides the environment jobs are launched in. Each compute job is run in the existing container via a `podman exec` command. Unfortunately, `podman exec` can become a bottleneck when many jobs are being launched for the same user simultaneously. We provide an API for interacting with exec sessions (IE, commands run by `podman exec`) including the ability to list them, inspect their status, and remove them. This means that each `podman exec` command must take a lock for the container in question, update the database with information about a new exec session that has been created, and then unlock the container; further database updates and locking of the container are necessary to update state when the exec session is started, and again when it finishes. The use of this exclusive lock and the amount of database I/O involved has been identified as a large part of the bottleneck with attempting to launch concurrent `podman exec` processes.

### Suggest potential solution

We want to introduce a new argument to `podman exec`, `podman exec --no-session`, which will eliminate database session tracking and minimize (but, likely, not entirely remove) use of the container lock. We would also like to eliminate the usage of `conmon` to monitor the exec session, and instead to directly invoke the OCI runtime from Podman. This should have further benefits by reducing time to start and minimizing resource consumption of exec sessions. Behaviorally, `podman exec --no-session` will act like a normal `podman exec` but will not perform writes to the database. The exec session will not be tracked as a result and will be inaccessible via API but this is acceptable for HPC usage. The `--no-session` flag should be implemented for local Podman only, and not the remote client, where it does not make sense. All flags of the `podman exec` command should be supported except `--detach` and `--detach-keys`. For implementation, I feel that this should be a new API - perhaps called `ExecNoSession` - implemented in `container_exec.go` in Libpod

comments.body: @mheon nice writeup.  do you have data on the perf hits? i think you need that to drive a solution and to determine where to start and eventually stop.

> We would also like to eliminate the usage of conmon to monitor the exec session, and instead to directly invoke the OCI runtime from Podman.

@mheon How should this work regarding passing through the stdio stream? Did you just want to leak the fds like `--log-driver passthrough-tty` does? AFAIK that is quite insecure when used with a tty because of TIOCSTI. I think like conmon we would have to allocate our own tty and proxy the io streams which then adds a quite a bit of complexity.
cc @giuseppe 


Also what happens if you kill the podman exec process, do we not leak the exec spec for the oci runtime permanently then?  Maybe we can unlink the files right away after the runtime command start the process to avoid the risk of them being leaked. But I guess since we already started doing session less for podman healthcheck that problem already exists, just that healthcheck still uses conmon. IMO whatever we decide here I think it would make sense for healthcheck to use the same code so we don't duplicate to much.

my understanding of the issue was that the cost is in the execution of Podman itself for short lived programs, and I am not sure how much of that we can cut if we go through Podman anyway.

On my machine, I get the following numbers (using `podman unshare true` as a lower bound for Podman):

```
➜ hyperfine 'podman unshare true' 'podman exec -l true' 'podman unshare crun exec d1098a6ebd47ff28c362146c63566e6bd20ade2b1eeebf281a02747e56776f8d true' 'crun exec d1098a6ebd47ff28c362146c63566e6bd20ade2b1eeebf281a02747e56776f8d true'
Benchmark 1: podman unshare true
  Time (mean ± σ):      23.1 ms ±   2.3 ms    [User: 14.7 ms, System: 13.9 ms]
  Range (min … max):    19.5 ms …  32.2 ms    123 runs

Benchmark 2: podman exec -l true
  Time (mean ± σ):      97.8 ms ±   7.2 ms    [User: 25.9 ms, System: 19.9 ms]
  Range (min … max):    87.5 ms … 126.1 ms    23 runs

Benchmark 3: podman unshare crun exec d1098a6ebd47ff28c362146c63566e6bd20ade2b1eeebf281a02747e56776f8d true
  Time (mean ± σ):      27.2 ms ±   2.0 ms    [User: 16.4 ms, System: 16.7 ms]
  Range (min … max):    23.3 ms …  33.6 ms    108 runs

Benchmark 4: crun exec d1098a6ebd47ff28c362146c63566e6bd20ade2b1eeebf281a02747e56776f8d true
  Time (mean ± σ):       4.6 ms ±   0.6 ms    [User: 1.1 ms, System: 3.6 ms]
  Range (min … max):     2.8 ms …   6.0 ms    555 runs

  Warning: Command took less than 5 ms to complete. Note that the results might be inaccurate because hyperfine can not calibrate the shell startup time much more precise than this limit. You can try to use the `-N`/`--shell=none` option to disable the shell completely.

Summary
  crun exec d1098a6ebd47ff28c362146c63566e6bd20ade2b1eeebf281a02747e56776f8d true ran
    5.03 ± 0.83 times faster than podman unshare true
    5.91 ± 0.91 times faster than podman unshare crun exec d1098a6ebd47ff28c362146c63566e6bd20ade2b1eeebf281a02747e56776f8d true
   21.26 ± 3.26 times faster than podman exec -l true
```

so if we take the fastest path to the OCI runtime, we could cut the cost to ~30% of what we have now, but still ~6 times slower than using directly crun.  Is that the expectation?  Is it enough?

> The use of this exclusive lock and the amount of database I/O involved has been identified as a large part of the bottleneck with attempting to launch concurrent `podman exec` processes.

do we have any number/reproducer to say so?  They would be useful to understand what we need to improve.

@giuseppe I think that sounds reasonable. Using the OCI runtime directly was AFAIK proving overly difficult, so having a Podman shortcut that performs substantially better than what we have right now should do what they need.

@Luap99 I was thinking it would work similar to passthrough logging, yes, but the `podman exec -t` case is interesting. We probably want to allocate a separate TTY for that and do standard stream copying - but that is probably too much for the initial PR. Not doing `-t` in the initial version seems sensible given this.

On healthchecks: I agree we should aim to share code there, the performance benefits of cutting Conmon out of the picture are substantial. But probably also not something for the initial implementation.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
